column7: message

>Why is it assumed that he would have asked a man? 

Sexism, basically. The man in that story is guilty of nothing more than being overly talkative, which can be annoying. Being too talkative is a minor flaw found in people of both sexes - some people just want to bang on about whatever they've been reading about that week. 

Normally the view of this would be just that - that its a bit annoying, but we just nod along and pretend to listen because some people are just like that. 

The warped view of men held by people like the above poster makes them see this as some weird sexist compulsion to explain: "I'm a man so I must know more than this mere *woman*". It can't just be someone who 'likes the sound of their own voice', it has to be stemming from a deep seated view that the woman doesn't know. "Men are sexist so this thing must be because of sexism, which proves men are sexist."

I think this is the core of the disagreement.  Why is it assumed that he would have asked a man?  If I want to share something with someone that I believe to be niche then I assume the person doesn't know about it, and if they do they will indicate in their response, or will stop me. Again, nothing to do with gender.  And frankly, explaining such innocuous behavior as the result of being a man is insulting and ignorant.  I'm not saying that mansplaining doesn't exist, but womansplaining happens equally often to me and trying to attach the blame to a single gender is stupid.
"@excited-conference, maybe you should try listening to your own voice for a change? 😉"

Sure, but some of the protesters have paid for tickets to the events, and aren't trespassing.

I wasn't aware about the service thing. Here's a link for others:

http://reason.com/blog/2016/03/03/protest-donald-trump-now-federal-crime

You've got to draw a distinction between protests, and the people crashing his events and disrupting them. Protests are fine and are protected in the constitution. The people who are crashing his events are just just trying to stop him from speaking, and play right into what his supporters are angry about.

column7: At what point does the man get to opt out? If the father backs the mother's decision until a week before the baby is born is he able to jump ship?

Financial abortion is bad because kids need money. We don't live in a society that leaves babies with no support. The idea is that if the father doesn't pay, the state is forced to, through entitlement programs. People don't want to pay taxes for other people's problems, especially if they think the problems are self-induced.

If a real abortion happens, the state doesn't have to pay anything. Taxpayers don't care. So it's not about fairness to the male gender or anything, it's all about the $$$.

"@piercing-engineer, I get your point about financial responsibility, but it's not just about money. It's about the emotional and time commitment too. A child changes your life forever, and it's not fair to expect someone to take on that responsibility if they don't want to. The father should have a say in this, even if it's late in the pregnancy."
